What the Headlines Say
Intro Data Power Shift Anxiety Two Camps Invisible Human Archetypes Modeling Limitations Try It All Figures

CS 210  ·  Data Management for Data Science  ·  Rutgers University  ·  Spring 2026

What the Headlines Say

A study of cognitive bias and agency framing across 179,372 AI news headlines, scraped, cleaned, and analyzed over 20 months from 13,019 publishers.

"AI Designs Computer Chips We Can't Understand" "AI threatens to eliminate millions of white-collar jobs" "Researchers deploy AI to accelerate drug discovery" "The AI model that wants to understand your mind"
179,372headlines
13,019publishers
20months
192search queries

Sadhana Vasanthakumar  CS 210 Final Project  ·  Rutgers University  ·  Spring 2026

01 / Introduction

What this project measures, and why it matters

Most people form their mental model of AI from news headlines, not research papers. A headline that says "AI Takes Over Another Industry" does something different from "Companies Adopt New AI Tools," even when the underlying story is identical. The first frames AI as an autonomous actor. The second describes a decision that humans made. At scale, that difference reshapes how readers think about accountability, risk, and control.

Is AI coverage increasingly treating AI as the actor, while the humans who build, fund, and deploy it quietly recede into the background?

That question became the Power Shift Index (PSI). A parallel question, how concentrated is anxiety in AI coverage, and does it vary by topic?, became the AI Anxiety Index (AAI). A third, when headlines mention humans, do they engage with human experience?, became the Invisible Human Framework. These are the three original metrics developed in this project. Five hypotheses were pre-registered. Three were supported. Two were not. The null results are reported here in full.

The data behind this: 179,372 headlines collected by running 192 search queries across nine topic tiers over 20 consecutive monthly windows. Each batch was deduplicated within its window and then again globally before being loaded into a normalized SQLite database. The NLP pipeline then scored every headline on sentiment, eight cognitive bias dimensions, agency framing, and the Invisible Human vocabulary. The full pipeline ran across four Jupyter notebooks from scraping through validation.

02 / The Corpus

How 179,372 headlines were collected

A nine-tier query architecture covered everything from core AI terms to named models to policy debates to human-impact stories. Every query ran against every monthly window, for a total of 3,840 API calls across 11 hours of runtime. Every headline was deduplicated within its window and then again globally before ingestion, bringing the raw count of 226,000 down to 179,372 unique records. Zero failed queries across the full run.

Volume grew +75% over the observation window. The Jan 2025 spike likely reflects DeepSeek and ChatGPT Gov announcements.

Publisher diversity grew alongside volume, a signal that AI coverage broadened beyond specialist tech press. The increasing share of policy, governance, and labor outlets is visible in the query-tier breakdown.

01 Finding One · The Power Shift Index

AI agency framing dominates, but the gap is narrowing

The Power Shift Index (PSI) measures the ratio of AI-as-agent headlines to human-controls-AI headlines. PSI = 100 means parity. The overall PSI across all 179,372 headlines is 733, AI is framed as an autonomous actor more than seven times as often as humans are shown controlling it.

But the trend is downward. Over 20 months, PSI declined at −10.6 points per month (R²=0.28, p=0.017). The gap is real, but it is narrowing. This likely reflects the growing volume of regulatory, research, and policy coverage, where humans, lawmakers, scientists, engineers, are necessarily in the foreground.

PSI  =  AI-agent headlines  ÷  Human-controls-AI headlines  ×  100
PSI = 100equal framing, AI-agent and human-agent appear at the same rate PSI > 100AI-agent framing dominates, AI appears as the actor more than humans do PSI = 733this corpus, AI is framed as an autonomous agent 7.3x more often than humans controlling it
Power Shift Index, monthly (Aug 2024 to Mar 2026)
Each point is one calendar month. The dashed section (Apr to Jun 2026) is a linear extrapolation of the observed trend, not a forecast. The shaded band shows the 95% confidence interval, which spans roughly 480 points, a reminder that month-to-month PSI is volatile.
When media says "AI decides," the humans who built and deployed that system quietly disappear from the sentence, and from the question of who is responsible.
Validation

PSI was validated by hand-labeling 200 stratified headlines drawn from across the 20-month window, comparing human judgment to the automated classifier. Cohen's kappa came out at 0.37, which falls in the "fair" range by the Landis and Koch scale. The main limitation is that keyword matching misses passive constructions like "a decision was made by the system" that a human reader would immediately recognize as AI-agent framing. For this reason, treat the absolute PSI figures as conservative lower bounds. The directional trend, the 10.6-point monthly decline over 20 months, is more reliable than any individual month's value, because trend direction is robust to systematic undercounting.

02 Finding Two · The AI Anxiety Index

Anxiety concentrates where expected, and leaks where it shouldn't

The AI Anxiety Index (AAI) is a composite score built from negative sentiment (VADER), fear keyword density, risk keyword density, and topic category flags. Mean AAI across the full corpus is 0.037 on a [0,1] scale.

The headline finding: Safety & Risk coverage carries an AAI of 0.093, compared to just 0.018 for Companies & Products. Cohen's d = +0.749, a medium-to-large effect. The anxiety concentration is real and statistically significant (p ≈ 0).

The subtler finding: even routine product announcements retain residual anxiety vocabulary. The broader news ecosystem seems to treat AI as inherently risk-adjacent, regardless of the story's actual topic. AAI trended upward across the full 20-month window, from ~0.034 to ~0.044, suggesting this anxiety spread is growing, not stabilizing.

AI Anxiety Index, monthly trend
Mean AAI per month (× 1000 for legibility). Consistent upward drift over 20 months.
Mean AAI by topic category
Health & Science and Safety & Risk carry the highest anxiety loads. Companies & Products is low but non-zero.
03 Finding Three · Cognitive Bias Co-occurrence

AI discourse has bifurcated into two non-overlapping camps

Eight cognitive bias dimensions were scored for each headline: fear, optimism, anthropomorphism, moral panic, agency attribution, techno-utopianism, economic threat, and control loss. When their co-occurrence patterns are mapped across 179,372 headlines, a clear structure emerges: all 28 pairs show strong positive correlations, ranging from r=0.62 to r=0.88. Emotionally charged headlines activate multiple bias dimensions simultaneously rather than triggering just one.

The strongest pairing is Moral Panic and Control Loss at r=0.88. Optimism and Techno-utopianism sit at r=0.86. The weakest is Agency Attribution and Economic Threat at r=0.62, still a strong positive correlation. Each pole of AI discourse brings its associated vocabulary along: breakthrough headlines carry techno-utopianism, optimism, and anthropomorphism together, while threat headlines carry fear, moral panic, control loss, and economic threat together. The nuanced vocabulary that sits between the two poles is what AI news consistently avoids.

Bias co-occurrence network, interactive
Drag nodes · scroll to zoom · hover for Pearson r · node size = mean bias score · edge thickness = correlation strength
Negative camp (fear, moral panic, control loss, economic threat)
Positive camp (optimism, techno-utopianism)
Mixed (anthropomorphism, agency attribution) Edge thickness = Pearson r correlation strength

The strongest co-occurrences: moral panic ↔ control loss (r=0.879), optimism ↔ techno-utopianism (r=0.862), fear ↔ moral panic (r=0.839). The language that would let you hold both possibilities at once, nuanced trade-off vocabulary, is the language the news ecosystem does not reach for.

04 Finding Four · The Invisible Human Framework

92.4% of AI headlines contain zero human-experience vocabulary

The Invisible Human Framework checks seven vocabulary domains that represent dimensions of lived human experience: grief and loss, spirituality and meaning, love and relationships, bodily experience, dignity and purpose, community, and childhood. Across 179,372 headlines, 92.4% score zero on every domain.

Even headlines that explicitly mention humans, workers, researchers, patients, students, engage these domains only 6.8% of the time. The absence is not topical; it is structural. AI news describes human consequences in economic and technical terms, not experiential ones.

Human-experience domain coverage: AI headlines vs. climate change control
AI corpus n=179,372. Climate change control corpus n=4,106. Same vocabulary pipeline applied to both.
What the control comparison found

To test whether 92.4% absence is actually specific to AI coverage rather than just how headlines work, we scraped 4,106 climate change headlines using the identical pipeline and applied the same seven-domain vocabulary. Climate change was chosen as the control because it shares key properties with AI news: it is a long-running multi-year story with mixed science, policy, economy, and human-impact coverage, and it draws from a comparable publisher mix. The result: climate headlines have a comparable any-domain coverage rate of 4.9%, versus 7.6% for AI. AI headlines score lower on Grief and Loss (0.54% vs 0.90%) and Community and Belonging, but higher on most other domains. This means the 92.4% absence is not a unique failure of AI journalism. It is a structural property of how news headlines compress information across topics. The more precise claim is that news headlines in general rarely engage the language of human experience, and AI coverage is consistent with that pattern. The two domains where AI headlines fall below even climate coverage, grief and community, are worth tracking as AI coverage continues to expand into human-impact territory.

05 Finding Five · Headline Archetypes

Four stable archetypes describe the AI headline landscape

K-means clustering on the full feature set (k=4, chosen by elbow + silhouette) identified four headline archetypes. The solution was validated for stability: mean ARI = 0.941 across 10 random seeds × 10 bootstrap subsamples × 10 feature subsets. The interpretive labels are defensible findings, not arbitrary names.

High Anxiety
24.4%
AAI mean: 0.052
43,731 headlines
Elevated fear, risk, and moral panic vocabulary. Safety, regulation, and existential risk stories.
Moderate Bias
38.0%
AAI mean: 0.038
68,132 headlines
The largest cluster. Mixed signals, product coverage with embedded concern, business reporting with hedged optimism.
Low Concern
25.2%
AAI mean: 0.027
45,176 headlines
Low emotional valence. Infrastructure, deployment, and implementation stories with neutral framing.
Neutral Reporting
12.5%
AAI mean: 0.025
22,333 headlines
Closest to factual reporting. Research results, technical releases, brief news items with minimal editorial framing.

Only 12.5% of AI headlines qualify as neutral. The remaining 87.5% carry detectable bias signal. That does not mean they are wrong, bias dimensions are not the same as factual error, but it does mean that most AI headlines are reaching for emotional framing beyond the literal facts.

Cognitive bias dimensions, mean scores across 179,372 headlines
Each bar is the mean keyword-presence score for that dimension across the full corpus, sorted highest to lowest. Techno-utopianism leads at 0.465, followed by moral panic at 0.412. Both the most-optimistic and most-fearful dimensions score above the midpoint, which is what the bifurcated-camps finding would predict.
06 Finding Six · Predictive Modeling

A classifier that beats chance by 6×. A regression that's honest about its limits.

The pruned decision tree (depth=5) predicts dominant bias type across 8 classes with 74.4% test accuracy, against a 12.5% random baseline. Train/test gap = 0.0008, the model generalizes cleanly, with no meaningful overfitting.

The regression story is more complicated. Ridge regression on AAI achieves R²=0.067 after removing features that have circular vocabulary overlap with the AAI components themselves. The inflated full-feature R² (0.22) was flagged, diagnosed, and corrected, removing the circular features reduced explained variance by 228.7%. The honest number is 0.067.

A permutation test (200 permutations) confirms the honest model captures real signal: empirical p=0.0. The features that survive the circularity correction, topic category, agency attribution score, VADER compound sentiment, and month number, do predict AAI, just not as strongly as the inflated model suggested.

On logistic regression's 99.5% accuracy

The logistic regression classifier achieves 99.46% test accuracy, but this is a tautology. The dominant_bias label is the argmax of the individual bias dimension scores; logistic regression predicts it from those same scores. The number is real but uninteresting. The decision tree (74.4%) is the honest performance figure, because it generalizes from features that do not directly define the label.

Confirmed
H2. Safety and Risk headlines carry significantly higher anxiety than product coverage
Cohen's d = +0.749, p near 0, a medium-to-large effect that held across all 20 months
Confirmed
H3. Named-entity headlines (GPT-4, Gemini, Claude) score lower on anxiety than generic AI headlines
Cohen's d = -0.133, p near 0, specific model names anchor coverage in concrete facts, reducing emotional framing
Reversed
H1. PSI was predicted to trend upward as AI becomes more prominent in coverage
Instead, PSI declined at 10.6 points per month (p = 0.017). The likely explanation: regulatory, policy, and researcher coverage grew faster than pure AI-as-actor stories, pulling the ratio downward. This is arguably a more interesting result than the original prediction.
Structural
H4. Headlines mentioning humans (workers, patients, researchers) were predicted to engage human-experience vocabulary more than others
Cohen's d = -0.032, p near 0 but effect is essentially zero. The absence of grief, dignity, and community language is not conditional on whether humans are mentioned, it is a property of how AI news is written regardless of subject matter.
Underpowered
H5. Major model release months were predicted to show elevated PSI
d = +0.85, p = 0.71. Only two months qualified as release months, which is far too small a sample to draw conclusions from. The effect size is large but the variance is enormous.
07 / Limitations

What this study cannot claim

AllSides coverage gap

AllSides political lean ratings matched only about 11–12% of the corpus by headline volume, covering roughly 27 of the 13,019 publishers scraped. Any analysis linking framing patterns to political orientation is preliminary and should not be read as representative of the full publisher landscape.

Keyword matching limits

PSI, AAI, and the eight bias dimensions all rely on surface-level keyword presence. Passive constructions — "a decision was made by the system" — read as AI-agent framing to a human but evade the lexicons. Cohen's kappa on a 200-headline hand-validation sample came out at 0.37, which falls in the fair range. Treat absolute index values as conservative estimates and directional trends as the more reliable signal.

Cross-validation divergence

When we ran VADER sentiment on the Figshare AI-news benchmark dataset as an external check, the compound score diverged from our corpus mean by roughly 0.08 units. We traced this to differences in source curation — the Figshare set skews toward longer feature articles, whereas ours is predominantly short news headlines. The divergence was documented as a finding rather than corrected away.

Scraping and sampling bias

Headlines were retrieved via Google News through the gnews library. GNews rank-orders results by recency and engagement signals, so popular outlets and recent stories are overrepresented. The nine keyword tiers were designed to stratify coverage, but inherently AI-adjacent topics were better sampled than edge cases. No claim is made about the 20-month window being a random draw from all AI journalism.

09 / Generated Figures

All notebook output figures

Every figure generated by Notebooks 02–04, in order. Click any image to enlarge. Descriptions explain what each chart shows and how to interpret it.

Power Shift Index — Monthly Trend (Aug 2024 to Mar 2026)
PSI measures AI-agent framing relative to human-controls-AI framing. PSI=100 is parity; the corpus sits at PSI=733 overall. The dashed reference line marks balance. The downward slope of 10.6 points per month (R sq=0.28, p=0.017) suggests regulatory, policy, and researcher coverage is pulling the ratio toward equilibrium over time. The Jan 2025 spike coincides with the DeepSeek and ChatGPT Gov announcements.
AI Anxiety Index — Monthly Mean
Mean AAI per month on the [0,1] scale. The index is a weighted composite of VADER negative sentiment, fear keyword density, risk keyword density, and topic-category flags. The upward drift from 0.034 in Aug 2024 to a peak near 0.044 in Feb 2026 shows anxiety language in AI coverage growing steadily, with a notable dip in Dec 2024 and acceleration through late 2025.
Cognitive Bias Dimensions — Mean Scores Across 179K Headlines
Mean keyword-presence score per bias dimension, sorted highest to lowest. Techno-utopianism leads at ~0.465 and economic threat is lowest at ~0.326. The chart shows both the most optimistic (techno-utopianism, optimism bias) and most fearful dimensions (moral panic, control loss) score above the midpoint, which is consistent with the bifurcated framing pattern found in the co-occurrence analysis.
Invisible Human Framework — Unconditional vs. Conditional Coverage
Comparison of domain coverage rates for all AI headlines (unconditional, purple) versus only those headlines that explicitly mention humans such as workers, researchers, or patients (conditional, orange). Even when filtered to human-mentioning headlines, coverage rates remain low across all seven domains. Hypothesis H4 predicted the conditional rates would be substantially higher; they are marginally higher for most domains but the effect is tiny (Cohen's d = 0.032), meaning the absence of human-experience vocabulary is not resolved by the presence of human subjects in the headline.
Invisible Human Framework — Monthly Coverage Across 20 Windows
Monthly coverage rates for each of the seven human-experience domains, Aug 2024 to Mar 2026. The y-axis runs to 5%, illustrating how persistently near-zero all domains remain. Childhood Play vocabulary (orange) is the only domain that consistently registers above 1%, largely driven by headlines about AI in education and child development. All other domains stay below 1.5% throughout the observation window, with no meaningful upward trend.
Cognitive Bias Co-occurrence — Pearson Correlation Matrix
Pearson r correlations between all pairs of bias dimensions across 179,372 headlines. All 28 off-diagonal values fall in the range 0.62 to 0.88, meaning every bias dimension co-occurs with every other at a high rate. The strongest pair is Moral Panic and Control Loss at r=0.88. The implication is that emotionally charged headlines tend to activate multiple bias dimensions simultaneously rather than one in isolation. Techno-utopianism and Optimism Bias co-occur at r=0.86, confirming the two poles identified in the findings.
Cognitive Bias Co-occurrence Network (28 Significant Correlations)
Network visualization of all 28 significant bias co-occurrences (all r > 0.62). Node size is proportional to mean bias score. Edge thickness and labeled weights show Pearson r. The graph is fully connected with no isolated nodes and no clear community separation, reinforcing that bias dimensions activate together rather than in separate camps. The densest connections cluster around Control Loss (connected to 7 others at r > 0.74) and Moral Panic.
Ridge Regression — Honest Feature Set (No Circular Features)
Left panel: predicted vs actual AAI scatter plot for the honest Ridge model after removing features with circular vocabulary overlap. R-sq = 0.067, MAE = 0.059. The predictions cluster near zero for low-AAI headlines and undershoot for high-AAI ones, reflecting the model's limited explanatory power after circularity correction. Right panel: the top feature coefficients. is_risk_category and bias_optimism_bias are the strongest positive predictors; bias_agency_attribution and vader_compound are the strongest negative ones. Removing circular features reduced R-sq by 228.7%.
Decision Tree Confusion Matrix — 8 Classes, Test Accuracy 74.4%
Confusion matrix for the pruned decision tree (depth=5) predicting dominant bias type. The diagonal shows correct classifications. Techno-utopianism (bottom-right, 22,327 correct) is the largest class and best predicted. Confusion concentrates between adjacent classes: moral panic is frequently confused with techno-utopianism and control loss, reflecting the high inter-class correlations. The 74.4% accuracy compares to a 12.5% random baseline across 8 classes.
Decision Tree Feature Importance (Gini)
Gini-based feature importance for the pruned decision tree. bias_techno_utopianism (0.290), bias_optimism_bias (0.265), and bias_moral_panic (0.250) are the three dominant features, accounting for roughly 80% of the tree's splitting decisions. bias_agency_attribution contributes essentially nothing (near zero), likely because it is already captured by other correlated dimensions.
K-Means Cluster Selection — Elbow and Silhouette
Left panel: within-cluster inertia for k=2 through k=9. The curve bends most sharply between k=3 and k=4, with diminishing returns past k=4, supporting the elbow selection. Right panel: silhouette score declines monotonically from k=2, but k=4 is selected as the best trade-off between interpretability and cluster cohesion. The dashed red line at k=4 marks the selected solution.
Headline Archetypes — K-Means Clusters in PCA Space (5,000-point sample)
PCA projection of a 5,000-headline stratified sample, colored by cluster assignment. PC1 explains 81.1% of variance and largely separates clusters along a bias-intensity axis. The four clusters are broadly separated: Neutral Reporting (blue, left) through Low Concern (orange), Moderate Bias (red), and High Anxiety (green, right). Some overlap between Moderate Bias and High Anxiety reflects the high inter-bias correlations seen in the heatmap.
PSI and AAI Trend Extrapolation — Apr to Jun 2026 (NOT a Forecast)
Linear extrapolation of the PSI and AAI trends three months beyond the observation window. Left panel: PSI extrapolated to roughly 630 to 650 by Jun 2026, with a 95% prediction interval spanning approximately 380 to 890. The interval is wide relative to the trend slope, a reminder that 20 monthly observations are insufficient for reliable forecasting. Right panel: AAI extrapolated to approximately 0.042. Both extrapolations assume trend continuity, which major AI news events can break in a single month.
Invisible Human Framework — AI Headlines vs. Climate Change Control Corpus
Side-by-side comparison of human-experience domain coverage rates for the full AI corpus (179K headlines, orange/red bars) versus a 4,106-headline climate change control corpus scraped with the identical pipeline (blue bars). AI headlines score lower than climate on Grief and Loss (0.54% vs 0.90%) and Community Belonging (0.31% vs 0.49%). For five of seven domains, AI coverage is at or above climate change rates. The any-domain rates are AI 7.6% vs climate 4.9%, confirming that the 92.4% absence rate is not a pathology specific to AI journalism but a structural feature of how news headlines work across topics.
